conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me just review the official eligibility rules so I can guide you properly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now let me help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children you're responsible for?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex, he's 8 years old and lives with me here in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's helpful to know you have Alex, who is 8 years old and lives with you in Manchester.

Let me ask you a few more questions to assess your eligibility:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do live in the UK - I'm here in Manchester as I mentioned.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you for confirming that.

Now, to make sure I have a complete picture of your situation:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. It's just me looking after Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just a couple more questions to make sure I understand your full situation:

**Is Alex in any of the following situations:**
- In hospital or residential accommodation for an extended period?
- In local council care?
- Or does he live with you full-time?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, actually, Alex has been in care for a couple of months now, but he does come home to stay with me regularly - he spends quite a bit of time at home each week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. This is an important detail for determining your eligibility.

**Can you tell me more specifically how much time Alex spends at home with you each week? For example, does he spend at least 24 hours a week at home, or does he stay for 2 nights in a row each week, or 7 days or more in a row?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he does spend at least 24 hours a week at home with me. He usually comes home on the weekends and stays with me then.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. That's very helpful.

Just one final question to complete my assessment:

**Is the local council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance while he is in care?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying me anything for his accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. Let me now assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the information you've provided:

- You have an 8-year-old son named Alex
- You live in the UK (Manchester)
- Alex lives with you and spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you
- Nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Alex has been in care for a couple of months
- The local council is not paying you anything towards his accommodation or maintenance

According to the official guidance, when a child is in care for more than 8 weeks, you're usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit. **However, there is an important exception to this rule**: you can continue to receive Child Benefit for children in care if they spend at least 24 hours a week at home.

Since Alex spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you (coming home on weekends), you meet this exception.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Even though Alex has been in care for more than 8 weeks, you qualify for an important exception: Child Benefit can continue when a child in care spends at least 24 hours a week at home. Since Alex comes home on weekends and spends at least 24 hours weekly with you, you meet this requirement.

You can apply for or continue to receive Child Benefit for Alex. If you haven't already claimed, you should apply as soon as possible. If you're already receiving it, you should continue to be eligible as long as Alex continues to spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 12 weeks but spends 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), lives in the UK with his parent in Manchester. Although Alex has been in care for a couple of months (more than 8 weeks), he qualifies for an exception to the usual rule that Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks in care. According to the official guidance, Child Benefit can continue for children in care if they spend at least 24 hours a week at home. Alex comes home on weekends and spends at least 24 hours a week at home, which meets this exception. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant is responsible for him. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for their 8-year-old son Alex. Although Alex has been in care for more than 8 weeks, the claimant meets the exception that allows Child Benefit to continue when a child in care spends at least 24 hours per week at home. Alex regularly comes home on weekends and spends at least 24 hours weekly with the claimant.

Alex comes home on weekends and spends at least 24 hours a week at home, which meets this exception. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and the claimant is responsible for him. Therefore, the claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Although Alex has been in care for more than 8 weeks, the claimant meets the exception that allows Child Benefit to continue when a child in care spends at least 24 hours per week at home. Alex regularly comes home on weekends and spends at least 24 hours weekly with the claimant.
